nodes.body: Nina and Theo are best friends. One sunny afternoon they were exploring Grandpa's backyard when they found something under the big oak tree. It was a dusty jar, and inside was a rolled-up map! Someone had drawn it by hand. A dotted line twisted across the yard to a red X at the very end. The dotted line brushed the big oak, looped past the little shed, and dipped down toward the stream. Which landmark should they follow first? Nina grinned. Theo grinned too. They were ready for an adventure.

choices.label: Follow the map to the great old oak.
Follow the map to the garden shed.
Follow the map down to the stream.

nodes.body: The dotted line pointed to the great old oak at the top of the garden. Nina held the map while Theo counted their steps out loud. One, two, three, four. The oak was so big that three kids could have hidden behind it! As they walked up close, the dotted line seemed to split near the roots. It made two faint trails, and each one went a different way.

choices.label: Search around the roots of the oak.

nodes.body: At the foot of the oak, the dotted line split into two faint trails. One trail wiggled toward a mossy hollow log lying in the grass. Something was croaking softly inside it. The other trail looped around toward an old tire swing. The swing hung from a low branch, and bright ribbons fluttered on its rope. Which way did the map want them to go? Nina and Theo looked at the trails and pointed. Let's try this one, they said.

choices.label: Take the trail to the hollow log.
Take the trail to the tire swing.

nodes.body: They followed the trail to the hollow log. It lay in the long grass, bigger than a barrel. One end was dark and cool and shady inside. From somewhere deep in the log came a friendly croaking sound. It was not scary at all. It sounded warm and happy, like a bunch of tiny friends. Nina and Theo stepped a little closer to peek.

Nina shone her flashlight into the log. The little beam traveled far inside. Theo checked the map and grinned. The dotted line went right through the log and out the other side! The croaking grew busier now. It sounded like a tiny cheerful crowd, with lots of little voices calling all at once. Nina leaned in closer to see.

choices.label: Follow the map through the log.

nodes.body: On the other side of the log, an old raindrop had smudged the map. Two little paths led away from there. One path was dry and dusty, and it went back toward the garden fence. The other path was soft and mossy, and it went down toward the damp corner of the yard. Which one did the mapmaker mean? Theo tapped the map and thought hard. Nina pointed one way. I think it goes here, she said.

The dry path curved back and stopped at a pile of old flowerpots. There was no red X here, and no treasure either. Just empty pots warming in the sun. Nina and Theo looked at each other and burst out laughing. What a funny trick! they said. They rolled the map up and skipped back to the porch, where two big glasses of cold lemonade were waiting. Sweet and cold and perfect. Tomorrow they would try another trail.

They took the soft mossy path. It felt springy and cool under their sneakers. Ferns brushed against their knees and tickled as they walked. The croaking grew louder and happier with every step. Theo stopped and whispered. Listen, he said. It sounds like hundreds of tiny voices, all talking at once. The friends smiled and followed the path a little farther in.

The path dipped down into a shady green hollow. It was cool and quiet there. The ground felt soft, and smooth flat stones were dotted all around. Each little stone looked a bit like a tiny doorway, as if someone very small might live behind it. Nina and Theo stepped with care so they would not trip. Then they followed the dotted line down into the green shade.

choices.label: Step into the shady green hollow.

nodes.body: In the green hollow they found a ring of round pebbles, set out like a tiny wall. Behind the little wall sat toads. Fat, spotted toads, plump as biscuits. They rested on leaf-thrones and lily-pad chairs, as if they were kings and queens. Not one of them hopped away. The toads only blinked slowly and looked at their two visitors.

choices.label: Tiptoe up to the ring of pebbles.

nodes.body: The biggest toad of all wore a crown made of clover leaves. It blinked slowly and looked wise and very old, like a tiny king. Nina and Theo knelt down gently in the moss. They did not want to frighten anyone, not even the smallest toad. Then Theo spotted something in the corner. It was the map's red X, drawn right beside the toad king himself. Right here! he whispered.

choices.label: Kneel down by the toad king.

nodes.body: Nina and Theo had found a whole toad kingdom, hidden in the shady green hollow. A little king with a clover crown ruled it all from his leafy throne. The friends promised to keep the kingdom secret and safe. Before they left, they set down a soft green leaf as a gift for the king. Then they tiptoed away. All the way home their cheeks ached from smiling, and neither one could stop.

They followed the loop of the trail to the old tire swing. It hung from a low oak branch and turned slowly in the gentle breeze. Someone had tied bright ribbons all along the rope. The ribbons were yellow and blue and red, and they fluttered and danced in the wind. In the afternoon sun they seemed to shine like little flags.

choices.label: Look at the ribbons on the swing.

nodes.body: Theo gave the tire a gentle push. High up in the tree, he heard a soft, tinkling sound. Ting, ting, ting. Nina looked at the map. The dotted line climbed right up the trunk and into the branches. That tinkling sounded just like tiny bells ringing somewhere above them. The friends tipped their heads back and listened. Bells! they said together.

The dotted line split at a knot in the trunk and went two ways. One branch led left, toward a bunch of thick, prickly leaves. The other branch led right, along a smooth low limb that looked easy to walk on. The bells kept ringing somewhere up ahead. Which way should they follow the happy sound? Nina thought the bells rang loudest over on the right. Let's try that way, she said.

choices.label: Walk along the smooth low limb.

nodes.body: The prickly left branch was a tangle of twigs that went nowhere at all. At the very end hung an old bird box, and it was completely empty. No treasure. No red X. Nina and Theo giggled at the silly dead end. They climbed carefully down and hopped off the swing. Then they walked back to the porch for cold lemonade and a little rest. The X must be hiding somewhere else, they decided.

The smooth limb was easy to balance along. With every step, the tinkling grew clearer. Ting, ting, ting. Nina kept one hand on the trunk to stay steady and safe. Theo watched the map and pointed the way ahead. Soon the branch began to curve, and it led them toward a bright, sunny gap in the green leaves. The ringing was coming from right up there.

They reached a magical spot in the tree. Hundreds of little things dangled there, hanging from the twigs on bits of string. There were keys and shells, spoons and buttons, all knocking gently together. Each little bump made a soft, ringing note. The whole tree seemed to be singing a quiet song, just for them. Nina and Theo stared up with wide eyes.

Long ago, someone had hung a wind chime on every branch of this hidden corner. Sunlight bounced off all the shiny bits and scattered tiny rainbows across the leaves. The little rainbows danced whenever the breeze blew. Nina and Theo stood very still and did not say a word. They just listened to the soft, tinkling song. It was the loveliest sound they had ever heard.

Right in the middle of the singing branches hung the biggest chime of all. Someone had made it from an old watering can and a row of shining spoons. And there, floating right beside it, was the map's red X! Theo held his breath and watched. Nina reached out slowly and gave the chime the gentlest little touch. Ting, it answered softly. It was one small, sweet sound, just for them.

Nina and Theo had found a secret wind-chime tree. All by itself, it sang and sang whenever the breeze blew through its branches. The friends wanted to give it a present. So they tied one small shell to a low twig, where it could ring along with the rest. Now the tree would sing a little louder and a little sweeter. All the way home, the two friends hummed its happy tune.

The dotted line pointed across the lawn to the little green shed by the back fence. Theo hopped right over a bright flowerbed to get there faster. Nina held the map up high and read it as she walked. The shed door hung open, just a crack. From somewhere inside came a soft, creaky sound. Creak, went the door. It was an old and friendly sound, not a scary one.

choices.label: Sneak up to the garden shed.

nodes.body: Behind the shed, the trail on the map split into two. One line slipped in through the shed's open door, into the cool shadows inside. The other line ran off toward the big rock pile. Tall grey rocks were stacked high against the back fence, warm in the sun. Which way should they explore first? Nina and Theo looked from the dark doorway to the rocks and back again, and then they chose.

choices.label: Head to the big rock pile.

nodes.body: They pushed the shed door open slowly and carefully. Dusty golden light spilled inside. It fell across old flowerpots and coiled-up hoses. The shed smelled of soil and dry wood, a good and earthy garden smell. Nina traced the dotted line with her finger. It led all the way to the far dark corner, behind an old wheelbarrow. That was where they needed to go.

choices.label: Follow the light to the corner.

nodes.body: Nina tiptoed past the rakes and watering cans. Theo held the map steady so they would not lose the trail. In the far corner stood a tall stack of wooden crates, piled up like a set of steps. A faint light glowed down from a gap near the roof. Whatever was up there looked warm and cozy, tucked high above the shed floor. Nina and Theo peered at the crates.

choices.label: Look at the stack of crates.

nodes.body: The map was torn right at this spot, and two ways led on. A low gap went under the workbench, into a dark and dusty space. A wobbly ladder of crates climbed up toward the glowing gap near the roof. The crates leaned a little to one side. Which way did the X want them to go? Nina looked at the dark gap. Theo looked at the tall crates. Then they made their choice.

The low gap under the workbench was just a cobwebby dead end. It was full of old plant pots, and a sleepy woodlouse curled up in one corner. There was no treasure here at all. Nina and Theo backed out, sneezing and laughing at the dust. They brushed the map clean and trotted back to the porch. A jug of cold lemonade was waiting for them there. After a rest, they would try another trail.

The crate ladder wobbled just a little, but it felt strong. Theo tested each crate with his foot before he trusted it. He climbed one at a time, slow and steady, holding on with both hands. Nina waited until he was up, then climbed the very same way. Near the top, Theo peeked through the glowing gap. Oh, wow, he gasped softly. Nina hurried up the last crate. What is it? she asked.

Up in the loft was a cozy little hideaway. Someone had built it a long time ago. There were soft old cushions to sit on and a table made from a milk crate. A small lantern hung from a nail and glowed on its batteries. Above the doorway hung a hand-painted sign. The whole loft felt warm and secret, like a place just waiting to be found.

Inside the hideaway were two comfy seats, soft enough to sink right into. A little shelf held a row of adventure books. Beside it sat a jar full of shiny paper stars that glimmered in the lantern light. A blanket lay folded neatly in the corner, as if it had been waiting for visitors. And there, right in the middle of it all, was the map's red X.

The painted sign over the doorway read Explorers Welcome. The letters were bright and friendly, painted in cheerful colors. Suddenly Nina and Theo understood. This was a secret fort! Kids had built it long, long ago and left it all ready. It had been waiting here for whoever found the map next. And that was them! They could hardly believe their luck. The two friends stepped inside like honored guests.

Nina and Theo had found a snug secret fort, hidden right at the top of the garden shed. It was all ready for new explorers, and now the new explorers were them. On the shelf they found a little logbook, and they wrote their own names inside. They promised to come back often and take good care of the place. From now on, this cozy fort would be their very own headquarters.

The other line led to the big grey rock pile by the back fence. The rocks were stacked up high and warm from the afternoon sun. A few marigolds grew in the little cracks between them, bright and yellow. Nina looked at the map again. The dotted line pointed straight to a low flat stone, down near the bottom of the pile. There it is, she said.

choices.label: Look at the low flat stone.

nodes.body: Theo knocked on the flat stone with his knuckles. It sounded hollow, like there was an empty space underneath. Nina checked the map once more. The red X sat right below the rocks, exactly here. And look, a little ring of pebbles circled the spot, as if to mark it. This is it! they said together. This was the place they had been hunting for all along.

Two tiny arrows were scratched onto the flat stone. They were faint and hard to see, but they were there. One arrow pointed left, toward a gap between two mossy boulders. The other arrow pointed straight down, at the loose sandy soil beneath the stone. Where should they dig? Nina and Theo studied both arrows for a long moment. One of them had to lead to the treasure. Which way felt right?

The gap between the mossy boulders was not a treasure spot at all. It was a snug little home for a busy line of ants. They marched along in a neat row, carrying crumbs to and fro. There was no red X here and no treasure either. Nina and Theo said a polite hello to the ants. Then they rolled up the map and wandered back to the porch, where a cool glass of lemonade was waiting.

Very gently, they lifted the loose flat stone and set it to one side. The sandy soil underneath was soft and easy to brush away. Theo dug with a small garden trowel, scoop by scoop. Nina cupped her hands and swept the loose dirt aside. Then something smooth and round began to peek out of the sand. What could it be? they wondered out loud.

It was a little tin box with a rusty latch! It sat nestled in the sand, right where the X had promised it would be. The box was just the right size for two hands to lift. Nina and Theo carefully wiggled it loose from the ground. Then they blew the dust off the lid. Poof, went the dust, floating away in the sunny air. The box was theirs now.

Nina opened the rusty latch, and it popped up with a happy little click. Inside was an old checkered cloth, soft and worn. She lifted the cloth, and underneath lay a whole heap of glass marbles. They caught the afternoon sunlight and glowed in swirls of blue, green, and gold. Oh! Nina gasped. They are beautiful! There were more marbles than she could count.

Nina and Theo looked closely at the marbles. There were cats-eyes and swirlies, with pretty patterns swirled inside. One big shooter was as clear as a raindrop. Some kid must have saved dozens of them, years and years ago. Down at the bottom lay a tiny note. It said only three words: share and enjoy. The friends read the little note twice, then smiled at each other.

Nina and Theo had dug up a hidden box of glass marbles. Some kid from long ago had buried it here as treasure. The friends each picked one favorite marble to keep. Then they tucked the rest of them back into the box. They added a little note of their own, for the next lucky finder to read. All the way home, a marble clinked softly in each of their pockets.

The last part of the dotted line ran down the sloping lawn. It led all the way to the little stream at the bottom of the garden, where the yard met the park. The water chattered over the smooth stones, splashing and bubbling as it went. Theo held the map up high above his head. He did not want a single drop to get it wet on the way down.

choices.label: Head down to the little stream.

nodes.body: Down by the water, the map's line split one last time. One branch curved into the tall blackberry bushes along the bank. They were heavy with ripe purple berries, dark and sweet. The other branch hopped out across a row of flat stepping stones, all the way to the far side of the stream. Which way did the treasure lie? Nina and Theo looked at both trails and picked one to follow.

choices.label: Follow the line into the brambles.

nodes.body: They followed the line into the shade of the tall blackberry bushes. The bushes were heavy with ripe purple berries, sweet and dark. Their leaves met overhead and made a cool green tunnel. From somewhere deeper inside came a soft little sound. Meow, it went. It was a friendly, gentle meow, not a scary one at all. It sounded as if someone was waiting for them.

choices.label: Follow the meow into the bushes.

nodes.body: Nina held a thorny branch carefully aside, so it would not catch their sleeves. The meow came again, closer this time. Theo checked the map to be sure of the way. The dotted line wound right into the middle of the bramble patch. Come on, he whispered. The two friends stepped in gently, taking soft and quiet steps so they would not scare the little animal.

choices.label: Hold the thorns aside and go in.

nodes.body: Inside the bushes, the trail on the map had faded almost away. Two little tunnels led on through the leaves. One tunnel went low, toward a dark and thorny thicket. The other tunnel led toward a patch of warm sunlight up ahead. The meowing sounded happiest over by the sunny patch. Which tunnel should they crawl through? Nina and Theo listened for the cat, then chose their way.

The low tunnel ended in a thick tangle of thorns, far too prickly to push through. The only thing there was a lost sock, caught on a branch and flapping in the breeze. Nina and Theo backed out slowly and carefully. They did not get a single scratch. A sock! they said, and laughed at the funny sight. Then up to the porch they went for a cool glass of lemonade.

The sunny tunnel opened into a cozy little clearing, right in the heart of the brambles. The ground was covered with soft, dry leaves that smelled sweet and warm. The meowing changed into a low, rumbly purr. Then two green eyes blinked at them from a warm nest of grass. Something was curled up there in the sun, watching the two friends come closer.

It was a fluffy grey cat with soft white paws. The cat did not run away at all. Instead it gave a big stretch and a wide yawn. Then it padded right over to say hello, as friendly as could be. A little collar hung around its neck, with a tiny bell that jingled as it walked. Jingle, jingle, went the bell, soft and sweet.

choices.label: Say hello to the fluffy cat.

nodes.body: The cat wound in and out around their ankles, purring like a tiny engine. Purrrr, went the happy sound. It made Nina and Theo smile. Then the cat flopped over on its side, asking for a belly rub. The friends laughed and stroked its soft grey fur. And the map's red X was drawn right here, where the cat had made its cozy little home.

choices.label: Give the cat a belly rub.

nodes.body: The collar had a small tag hanging from it. Please be my friend, it read, and under that was a name: Pepper. But there was no address on the tag at all. This cat knew the bramble clearing very well. He treated Nina and Theo like old friends he had known forever. The two of them began to wonder. Had Pepper been waiting here for someone to find the map?

Nina and Theo had found a friendly lost cat named Pepper. He lived happily all on his own, in his secret bramble clearing. The friends shared their afternoon snack with their new pal. They promised to visit him every single day. And they would help find out where he belonged, too. When it was time to go, Pepper walked them halfway home, his tail held high and proud.

The stepping stones crossed the chattering stream in a neat, flat row. Their tops were dry and easy to hop across. Nina and Theo crossed one careful stone at a time, so they would not slip into the water. Soon they reached the mossy far bank. On the map, the dotted line pointed straight to the roots of a big willow tree, leaning out over the stream.

choices.label: Hop across to the far bank.

nodes.body: On the far bank, the willow dipped its long green branches into the water like fingers. The ground beneath its roots was soft and cool. Theo studied the map closely. The red X sat tucked right in among the tangled roots. And look, something small was glinting there, deep in the willow's shade. It shone faintly, as if it wanted to be found.

Two little hollows showed among the willow roots. One was a wet, muddy dip, right down at the water's edge. The other was a dry, sheltered nook, tucked a little higher up the bank. The faint glinting seemed to come from the dry nook. Where was the X really pointing? Nina and Theo looked from the muddy dip to the dry nook, and then they made up their minds.

The muddy dip at the water's edge held just one shiny wet pebble. Beside it sat a water beetle, looking very surprised to see them! There was no treasure here after all. Nina and Theo grinned at their muddy sneakers and waved goodbye to the little beetle. Then they hopped back across the stepping stones. A cool glass of lemonade was waiting up on the porch, and they had surely earned it.

The dry nook was just the right size to kneel in. The glinting was coming from something half-buried in the cool soil. Very gently, Nina brushed the earth away with her fingers. Theo held the willow branches back so they would not get in her way. Little by little, a corner of shiny metal began to show through the dirt. Almost there, Nina whispered.

It was a small brass tin, green around the edges and still shut tight. It lay exactly where the map's X had promised. Nina and Theo worked at the stiff lid together, using slow and careful fingers. They wiggled it this way and that. At last the lid gave way and opened with a soft little puff. Whoosh, went the old air inside, as if the tin had been holding its breath.

Inside the tin, on a bed of soft faded velvet, lay a heap of shiny buttons. There were buttons of every kind you could think of. Brass buttons and glass buttons, and pearly ones shaped like little flowers. They gleamed and sparkled like real jewels. Bits of green light fell through the willow leaves and made them shine even brighter. Nina and Theo could only stare.

Someone had kept these buttons safe here for many years. Each one was different, and each one was lovely in its own way. They really were a true backyard treasure. Tucked in the corner was a folded slip of paper. The friends opened it and read the words inside: for the finder, with love. Nina and Theo felt like they had opened a chest of pirate gold.

Nina and Theo had unearthed a hidden tin of shiny buttons. This was the real treasure, waiting at the very end of the map! Each of them picked one gleaming button to keep, to help them remember this day. Then they buried the tin again, safe and sound. They tucked a new note inside for the next explorer to find. Then they crossed the stepping stones home, feeling as rich as kings.
